ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese Phở Gà; closest defensible match is 'chicken noodle soup' (FOODON_03311069). Phở Gà is a Vietnamese rice-noodle soup with sliced chicken in spiced bone broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Phở Gà code. 'Chicken soup' (A00GJ) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. The dish uses rice noodles and a spiced Vietnamese broth rather than a generic Western chicken soup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Phở Gà or Vietnamese chicken noodle soup as of 2026.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (Thai basil, coriander, spring onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Sliced Chicken Pho is approximately 500 g, comprising roughly 150 g rice noodles, 80 g sliced chicken, 250 g broth, and 20 g fresh herb and bean sprout garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Pho, chicken (Phở gà) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per-100g nutrient fields. Values are accepted as sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Phở Gà. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central chicken noodle soup component data (SR Legacy #06410) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice noodles, boiled (analogous estimate for rice noodle component of Phở Gà)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Phở Gà as a composite dish. GI of 61 is assigned based on the GI of boiled rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables, which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. The broth and chicken components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (61 × 50.4) / 100 ≈ 30.7 g (carbohydrate per serving = 10.08 g/100g × 500 g = 50.4 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 290 mg/100g; broth and fish sauce are primary sodium drivers; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1,450 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the 1,500–2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit broth consumption, avoid adding hoisin sauce or sriracha at the table, and request reduced fish sauce in broth seasoning to keep total sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles carry a medium-to-high GI (~61–70) and a 500 g serving delivers approximately 50 g carbohydrate with only 4 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of ~30.7 g which is clinically significant for postprandial blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion with extra sliced chicken and additional bean sprouts or fresh herbs to lower the glycemic load per meal, and avoid adding hoisin sauce which contributes additional sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 290 mg/100g (approximately 1,450 mg per 500 g serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern; total fat is moderate at 2.99 g/100g and saturated fat is low at approximately 0.8 g/100g, which is favourable for lipid profiles.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit broth intake to reduce sodium burden, choose lean chicken breast over thigh, and avoid high-sodium condiments such as hoisin sauce and sriracha to keep the dish within heart-healthy dietary parameters.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean chicken is low in arachidonic acid; broth spices (ginger, star anise, cinnamon) provide anti-inflammatory gingerols and polyphenols; saturated fat is approximately 0.8 g/100g, well below the 5 g/100g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Sliced Chicken Pho in moderation, limiting broth volume to reduce sodium, avoiding chilli and hoisin sauce condiments, and monitoring individual tolerance to nightshade garnishes such as chilli which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sliced Chicken Pho (Phở Gà) is a Vietnamese rice-noodle soup made by simmering chicken bones with aromatic spices — star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and charred ginger and onion — to produce a clear, fragrant broth that is seasoned with fish sauce and salt. Flat rice noodles (bánh phở) are blanched and placed in a bowl, topped with thinly sliced poached or roasted chicken breast or thigh, and the hot broth is ladled over. The dish is served with a garnish plate of fresh Thai basil, bean sprouts, lime wedges, sliced chilli, and hoisin and sriracha sauces on the side. Per 100 g, Phở Gà provides 90 kcal, 5.48 g protein, 2.99 g fat, 10.08 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g dietary fibre, and 290 mg sodium, as reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo database. A standard 500 g restaurant bowl delivers approximately 450 kcal and 50 g carbohydrate, creating a glycemic load of approximately 30.7 g based on the medium GI (~61) of boiled rice noodles. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes due to its sodium content and carbohydrate load from rice noodles, but is relatively low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it a lighter noodle soup option compared to beef pho or laksa. It is widely consumed in Vietnam and among Vietnamese diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically as a breakfast, lunch, or dinner dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of pho ga?
What is the GI of pho?
Can I eat pho with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all fields. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy chicken noodle soup data as HPB does not separately report this value. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the standard approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #06410 (chicken noodle soup, canned, condensed) as the closest approved-source analogue, as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. This is documented as an adapted value. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this traditional Vietnamese preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish entry; polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish entry; trans_fat_g — trans fat data not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_c_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b6_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b12_mcg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; folate_mcg_dfe — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_d_mcg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_e_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; phosphorus_mg — mineral not reported by HPB for this dish entry; zinc_mg — mineral not reported by HPB for this dish entry; selenium_mcg — mineral not reported by HPB for this dish entry; water_g — moisture data not reported by HPB for this dish entry. Source consulted and confirming unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/, accessed 2026-04-16). 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct composite GI measurement for Phở Gà exists in an approved source. 4. Halal status is assessed as likely true given chicken and fish sauce ingredients, but individual restaurant preparation should be verified. 5. The dish is Vietnamese in origin and is consumed widely in Singapore; multilingual coverage includes Vietnamese (Phở Gà) and Chinese (鸡肉河粉 / 雞肉河粉) as the primary non-English forms relevant to the Singapore context. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Vietnamese dish, not a Singaporean dish, and the G6 multilingual rule for Vietnamese dishes requires only the Vietnamese native-script name.
